text: "Intelligence" is an ill-posed concept. It conflates at least four things.

raw.full_text: "Intelligence" is an ill-posed concept. It conflates at least four things.

text: 1.  Ability to learn, and thus to modify oneself: metacognition.

raw.full_text: 1.  Ability to learn, and thus to modify oneself: metacognition.

text: 2.  Functionality. If it works, it’s Smart. If it doesn’t work it’s dumb.  Tautological.

raw.full_text: 2.  Functionality. If it works, it’s Smart. If it doesn’t work it’s dumb.  Tautological.

text: 3.  A sort of “might makes right” intellectual firepower.  This is real and everyone recognizes it.  But part of what it entails is that a smarter person with a false belief can overpower a dumber person with a true belief.

raw.full_text: 3.  A sort of “might makes right” intellectual firepower.  This is real and everyone recognizes it.  But part of what it entails is that a smarter person with a false belief can overpower a dumber person with a true belief.

raw.full_text: Check this out bro.  The ribcage expands.  How?  Muscles contract.  They can only pull.  They can't push.  How dafuq can your body take the ball that is your torso, and make it *bigger,* when the surface of that ball is nothing but pulling muscles? https://t.co/TzBCnAOifB

text: Also notice, your neck muscles and various others like the pec minor connect to the upper ribs too.  So if you try to expand your *upper* ribcage especially, your neck tenses up.  Because those muscles got recruited to help lift the ribs.  Feel that?  Cool, huh. https://t.co/OcjzCW14p4

text: It’s striking me how there are a *lot* of different modes of storytelling, and they have *amazingly* little to do with each other.

raw.full_text: It’s striking me how there are a *lot* of different modes of storytelling, and they have *amazingly* little to do with each other.

text: Here’s the main ones I’m seeing right now.

raw.full_text: Here’s the main ones I’m seeing right now.

text: 1.  The therapy mode.  You really don’t care about the listener at all.  You’re telling your story … essentially to yourself, in order to make sense of it, but someone else might be present.

raw.full_text: 1.  The therapy mode.  You really don’t care about the listener at all.  You’re telling your story … essentially to yourself, in order to make sense of it, but someone else might be present.

text: You keep telling the story until you find a version that fits somehow, and THEN you keep telling THAT until you’ve worked out its emotional charge.   And then on to the next big event.  If the listener likes you, they may indulge you, if not, you may be insufferable.

raw.full_text: You keep telling the story until you find a version that fits somehow, and THEN you keep telling THAT until you’ve worked out its emotional charge.   And then on to the next big event.  If the listener likes you, they may indulge you, if not, you may be insufferable.

text: 2.  The downplaying mode.  You’re basically trying to drain all the drama out of the story in order to give the listener as much *leeway* as possible in interpreting it.

raw.full_text: 2.  The downplaying mode.  You’re basically trying to drain all the drama out of the story in order to give the listener as much *leeway* as possible in interpreting it.

text: This can be done reflexively in high-distance cultures, as a form of politeness, or you might do this in order to find and recognize a rare person who thinks like you.  You’re checking to see whether the listener *can tell* which beats of the story are notable.

So, as one of my farsighted software wizard friends informed me, the way to prevent your software from breaking due to "dependencies" is to include all the dependencies in the program itself.  

Control the dependencies --> make it bulletproof.

raw.full_text:
So, as one of my farsighted software wizard friends informed me, the way to prevent your software from breaking due to "dependencies" is to include all the dependencies in the program itself.  

Control the dependencies --> make it bulletproof.

text:
To accomplish this, you "build" what's called a "static executable file."  

"Static executable file" means it doesn't need dependencies installed on your computer to run—they're all built into the program itself.

raw.full_text:
To accomplish this, you "build" what's called a "static executable file."

The platform encompasses *both* the graphical user interface you see on your screen, *and* the deep architecture that translates software languages into machine code.

text: Read an utterly cursed manual this morning, for a gadget full of demonic software cruft, and it made apparent one reason manuals are poorly written, which is this: there's no coherent audience.

raw.full_text: Read an utterly cursed manual this morning, for a gadget full of demonic software cruft, and it made apparent one reason manuals are poorly written, which is this: there's no coherent audience.

text: The audience for manuals is bifurcated.  On the one hand, they are written for the most moronic conceivable end-user, a person without common sense to begin the diagnostic process themselves.  "Is there fuel in the tank?" level stuff.

raw.full_text: The audience for manuals is bifurcated.  On the one hand, they are written for the most moronic conceivable end-user, a person without common sense to begin the diagnostic process themselves.  "Is there fuel in the tank?" level stuff.

text: Ironically, market forces conspire to make this most helpless possible end-user into the most desirable possible customer: he won't give you pushback or cause trouble.  He's an infinite sink of indiscriminate gadget spending.

raw.full_text: Ironically, market forces conspire to make this most helpless possible end-user into the most desirable possible customer: he won't give you pushback or cause trouble.  He's an infinite sink of indiscriminate gadget spending.

text: Therein lie the roots of the cursed Infantilization Spiral, leading to the technological hell realm we currently find ourselves mired in.

text: The OTHER people who read manuals are technicians: the most capable, jaded and agentic possible users.  These are people who have internalized an acronym, RTFM.

text:

Is the manual a document intended to enlighten a free and capable people?  To describe the workings of the mechanical system as clearly as possible?

text: Big chunk of worldview that I often reference, but not sure I've tweeted about before, is an inversion of assumptions.

text: A normal assumption in human societies is that you can assemble a group of people together to achieve a task, a common purpose.

raw.full_text: A normal assumption in human societies is that you can assemble a group of people together to achieve a task, a common purpose.

text: This is the presupposition behind teams, armies, companies, schools, hospitals, governments, most everything we do in civilization.

raw.full_text: This is the presupposition behind teams, armies, companies, schools, hospitals, governments, most everything we do in civilization.

text: This assumption is so natural it should not need to be explained, but it does now, because of the inversion of assumptions.

text:
Assumption 1:  People are valuable.
Assumption 2:  People are general problem-solving agents.
Assumption 3:  A team can be greater than the sum of its parts.

text: The inversion of assumptions takes for granted that existing institutions inevitably chug along on their own, and sees human participation in them as pure downside.

text: Theres tons of problems with this view, but the biggest one is: institutional momentum may *seem* to derive from mechanical laws and processes, but it actually comes from the fact that *a whole lot of individual people* are invested in making sure the institution keeps going.

text: Man gets lost in symbols all the time.  If it is our nature to be a symbol-making species, then it follows that we should carry this susceptibility: to reliably turn towards symbols, and away from reality.

raw.full_text: Man gets lost in symbols all the time.  If it is our nature to be a symbol-making species, then it follows that we should carry this susceptibility: to reliably turn towards symbols, and away from reality.

text: The natural world does not *need* symbols to do what it does.  And yet it becomes a source of symbols: the tree, the lion, the rock, the sun, the flower, the river.

raw.full_text: The natural world does not *need* symbols to do what it does.  And yet it becomes a source of symbols: the tree, the lion, the rock, the sun, the flower, the river.

text:
We, on the other hand, do need symbols to do what we do,
which is coordinate.

raw.full_text:
We, on the other hand, do need symbols to do what we do,
which is coordinate.

text: Once you notice this, when you start to realize what symbols *are,* you grow suspicious.

raw.full_text: Once you notice this, when you start to realize what symbols *are,* you grow suspicious.

text: Symbols can be distorted in ways that reality cannot.

raw.full_text: Symbols can be distorted in ways that reality cannot.

text: The suspicion is: if you study the symbols on their own terms, you're just studying, or rather *internalizing* whatever ways those symbols have been distorted in the past by whoever manipulated them.

raw.full_text: The suspicion is: if you study the symbols on their own terms, you're just studying, or rather *internalizing* whatever ways those symbols have been distorted in the past by whoever manipulated them.

text: And within the world of symbols, there's no way out of this trap.  It's symbols all the way down.

raw.full_text: And within the world of symbols, there's no way out of this trap.  It's symbols all the way down.

text: The question becomes: at what level do you start mistrusting symbols?  At the level of myth, culture, ideology?  At the level of sentence?  At the level of words themselves?  At the level of images and associations?

raw.full_text: The question becomes: at what level do you start mistrusting symbols?  At the level of myth, culture, ideology?  At the level of sentence?  At the level of words themselves?  At the level of images and associations?

raw.full_text:
Spent some time reading late Aldous Huxley essays this morning.  Towards the end of his life, 1958, post-success of Brave New World.  

William Whyte's "The Organization Man" is hot new stuff, Marshall McLuhan hasn't really hit the scene yet.

text:
He's reckoning with propaganda, and how it manifests in democratic societies and dictatorships.  Using a lot of Thomas Jefferson as jumping-off points.

He sees mass media and propaganda as a "can be used for good or ill" kind of thing, which is disappointing to me.

raw.full_text:
He's reckoning with propaganda, and how it manifests in democratic societies and dictatorships.  Using a lot of Thomas Jefferson as jumping-off points.

He sees mass media and propaganda as a "can be used for good or ill" kind of thing, which is disappointing to me.

text: There's a lot of ... aggrandizing the ... temperament of the "intellectual," you know, the individual is capable of reason and reflection, but propaganda works on the lower natures of crowds.  To be in a crowd is to be "herd-poisoned."
